title: Hard Wart-Like Growth on a Cat’s Head: Causes and Care
meta_desc: Found a hard bump on your cat's head? Learn about common causes like cysts or skin tags and why these growths are usually not emergencies.
content_html:
What is This Hard Growth?

Discovering a hard, wart-like bump on your cat's head can be unsettling, but these growths are often benign. In many cases, what looks like a wart may actually be a sebaceous cyst (a clogged oil gland), a cutaneous horn (a buildup of keratin), or a common skin tag. While feline papilloma virus can cause actual warts, they are less common in cats than in dogs and often appear in older or immunocompromised pets.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a hard, non-painful growth is generally Low. If the bump is not actively bleeding, oozing pus, or causing your cat to scratch excessively, it is typically not a medical emergency. You should monitor the area and schedule a routine veterinary exam to have the mass identified. However, if the growth suddenly changes shape, size, or color, it is best to move your appointment up to rule out more serious conditions like basal cell tumors.

How Photos Help Your Vet

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the growth is a vital part of the triage process. A photograph allows your veterinarian to examine the texture and attachment point of the bump before you even arrive at the clinic. It also provides a visual baseline so you can accurately track whether the growth is enlarging or changing over time, which is the most critical information your vet needs to determine if a biopsy is necessary.

faqs.question: Can I squeeze the bump to see what is inside?
faqs.answer: No, you should never squeeze or attempt to pop a growth on your cat. This can lead to deep tissue infection, significant pain, and can make it harder for a vet to provide an accurate diagnosis.

faqs.question: Are these growths common in older cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, as cats age, they are more prone to developing various skin lumps, including benign cysts and skin tags, which are often harmless but should still be monitored.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about a skin growth?
faqs.answer: You should seek veterinary care more quickly if the growth is growing rapidly, appears red and inflamed, starts bleeding, or if your cat seems bothered by it.
